data.title: Hims & Hers: Ramping Up A New Billion-Dollar Segment (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS) in February, I downgraded it to Hold. Despite much volatility in the stock price since then, the stock has been underperforming the S&P 500 (SP500) during this period of time.

The company announced the launch of its Menopause and Perimenopause specialty yesterday, sending the stock up 16%. This specialty is part of its “Hers” segment, which currently consists of half a million subscribers. Although the company does not break out its revenues by “Hims” and “Hers” separately, management estimates that the expansion of this specialty will enable them to surpass $1B in revenue for “Hers” in 2026, which would roughly amount to 36% of total estimated revenue.

The way I see it, the announcement is very timely, as the stock has been range-bound for a long period of time, especially after the termination of its partnership with Novo Nordisk (NVO). Although the management heavily emphasized building a full-spectrum, vertically integrated, personalized healthcare platform, the company saw its revenues in Q2 FY 2025 decline sequentially for the first time since going public, driven by softness in demand for its compounded GLP-1.

However, the growth rate in personalized treatments remained strong. In the meantime, Hims & Hers is taking a long-term view where it is making strategic capital investments and acquisitions to expand into new specialties as well as across geographies in the coming years.

Plus, what caught my attention was the magnitude of difference in revenue estimates between consensus and management’s FY 2030 projections. Coupled with a short float of 35%, the timing of the new specialty launch in “Hers” could just provide the stock the much-needed re-rating that I believe it deserves.

As a result, I will upgrade my rating on the company to a Buy with an updated price target of $80 per share, representing an upside of 30% from its current levels.

Expanding “Hers” to Achieve $1B In Revenue in 2026

Hims & Hers announced the launch of a Menopause and Perimenopause specialty in women’s health yesterday. At the moment, “Hers” serves over half a million subscribers, and by expanding their offerings in Menopause & Perimenopause, Hers is now on track to surpass $1B in annual revenue in 2026.

To put things in perspective, the company has a total of 2.4M subscribers that were reported on their Q2 FY 2025 earnings. This means that their share of women subscribers currently stands at roughly 20.8%. While the company does not break their revenue by “hims” and “hers” (men and women separately), consensus estimates currently point to a total revenue of $2.79B by FY 2026. Therefore, assuming that Hers's revenue surpasses $1B, it will account for 36% of Total revenue by FY 2026.

The announcement of the launch is very timely as the company tries to shield its revenue from GLP-1-related volatility by launching new offerings in hormone health, lab testing, and longevity-focused care and positioning itself as a “personalized health and wellness platform.”

The Timing Couldn’t Have Been Better

In the previous shareholder letter, the management discussed at length their vision of building a “next-generation” healthcare platform by placing customers at the center of every decision.

In fact, I believe that the following slide from their earnings presentation captures their value proposition quite accurately, as they move beyond simply connecting customers to providers to delivering truly personalized care.

Think of it as the Hims & Hers flywheel effect, where more subscribers lead to more proprietary data that allows providers to make more informed decisions, leading to superior customer experience, which in turn fuels even more subscribers onto the platform.

Yet, the stock has remained range-bound for quite a while, especially after the termination of its partnership with Novo Nordisk.

While I am not going to get into the details of why the partnership was terminated in the post, it hurt investor sentiment when Hims & Hers reported its first sequential revenue decline in Q2 FY 2025 since it went public.

During the quarter, Hims & Hers reported a year-over-year revenue growth of 73% to $545M, missing estimates by 1.3%. One of the main reasons is tied to its compounded GLP-1 revenue, which fell 17.39% sequentially to $190M, accounting for 34.8% of Total revenue.

However, if we look at the details, the underlying fundamentals at Hims & Hers still remain quite strong. The company ended the quarter with 2.4M subscribers, growing 31% YoY. Nearly 70% of the new subscribers who joined the platform are utilizing a personalized treatment, while their total count of subscribers on a personalized treatment grew 89% YoY, with average revenue per subscriber growing 30% YoY to $74.

In the meantime, the company plans to add lab testing and expand into longevity-focused care in 2026, along with spearheading its growth opportunities internationally with its recent acquisition of a leading digital healthcare provider called ZAVA Global. The company estimates at least $50M in revenue from ZAVA through the remainder of FY 2025 at a positive adjusted EBITDA margin as they expand their presence in the UK, Germany, France, and Ireland.

In my earlier posts on Hims & Hers, I had often said that the company is more than just a compounded GLP-1 provider but more of a vertically integrated healthcare infrastructure. While I strongly believe that to be the case as of this writing, the truth is short-term investor sentiment seems to be heavily correlated to the volatility of the news surrounding GLP-1s. Last month, Hims & Hers shares declined when the US FDA issued a warning letter over its marketing of compounded semaglutide, the weight loss drug marketed under the brand name Wegovy by Novo Nordisk.

While it is not uncommon for pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies to receive warning letters from the FDA for various reasons, it has certainly not helped Hims & Hers stock for several months now.

However, with the company launching new offerings in Hims last month and expanding its specialty with menopause and perimenopause in “Hers,” it could give investors the required confidence in the management’s vision in building a full-spectrum healthcare platform and reaching its long-term targets that we will discuss in the next section.

Some Short-Term Patience Is Required

Aside from revenues sequentially declining in Q2, one of the other reasons investor sentiment was dampened was because of its mixed picture in profitability.

While the adjusted EBITDA margin grew 300 basis points on a year-over-year basis to 15%, their operating and free cash flow turned negative, as can be seen below.

This was driven by working capital investments and an increase in capex to deliver superior personalization and broader capabilities of their verticalized pharmacy structure.

However, the way I see it, the company reiterated their long-term 2030 revenue and adjusted EBITDA targets of $6.5B and $1.3B, respectively. This would require the company to grow their revenues and adjusted EBITDA at a CAGR of 22.5% and 33%, respectively, over the next five years based on management’s updated FY 2025 guidance.

However, markets are grossly underestimating management’s FY 2030 projections. Current consensus estimates point to Hims & Hers delivering a total revenue of $4.21B by FY 2030, which is 35% below management’s projections.

In the image above, you can also see that the company has received a storm of downward revisions to their forward revenue growth estimates on a 3M basis, which coincides with the timing of the cancellation of their Novo Nordisk partnership.

With the magnitude of difference between consensus estimates and management projections for FY 2030, it provides the opportunity for the stock to be rerated significantly higher, should management successfully demonstrate that it can deliver the targets it has set.

By launching its new specialty in “Hers,” Hims & Hers will unlock a whole new TAM (total addressable market) with the opportunity for subscriber and revenue acceleration from here onwards. This should be followed by the launch of lab testing and longevity-focused care next year, as management has promised. When investors see faster than expected growth in subscribers and personalized treatment, their confidence in management’s vision and long-term targets will return.

Revisiting my valuation

In the earlier section, I discussed that consensus is heavily underestimating management’s FY 2030 revenue projections, leaving room for Hims & Hers stock to be rerated higher should the company indeed deliver.

One of the other potential tailwinds that Hims & Hers has is the unusually high short float of 35%, which could lead to outsized volatility in the share price when investor confidence returns.

For now, I will be conservative and take forward consensus estimates into account while estimating my price target for Hims & Hers. Over the next three years, consensus estimates point to Hims & Hers generating $3.3B in revenue, growing in the high teens between FY 2026 and FY 2027.

When it comes to profitability, management currently estimates the company to generate an adjusted EBITDA margin of 13.5%. Over the following two years, I will assume that it will be able to unlock at least 100 basis points of operating leverage every year to 15.5% by FY 2027, as it makes progress towards its FY 2030 goals. This will translate to an adjusted EBITDA of $511M by FY 2027, which is equivalent to a present value of $402M when discounted at 8%.

Taking the S&P 500 as a proxy, where its companies grow their earnings at an average rate of 9% every year with a price-to-earnings multiple of 15-18, I believe that Hims & Hers deserves a premium of 2.5 times that of the S&P 500 given the projected growth rate in its earnings. This will translate to a P/E ratio of 45, or a price target of $80 per share, representing an upside of 30% from its current levels.

Risks and Conclusions

The launch of the new specialty in “Hers” could be the spark that fuels a renewed investor confidence in the company, as it allows it to generate revenues via unlocking TAMs in new specialties that it has planned for in 2026.

In all of this, the macroeconomic environment could potentially turn out to be a headwind, as it can derail consumer spending on the platform.

Having said that, I believe the potential for rerating is significant given the difference in estimates between consensus and management as well as the large short float.

Given the attractive risk-reward at this point, I will upgrade my rating to a Buy with a price target of $80 per share, representing an upside of 30% from its current levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Chair Powell's New Revelations On Monetary Policy
data.description:
Chair Powell Named Adam Smith Award Recipient

On Wednesday, Jerome Powell was named the 2025 Adam Smith Award recipient by the National Association for Business Economics [NABE].

According to their website, “NABE is the premier professional association for business economists and others who use economics in the workplace.”

Their primary mission is “to advance the practice of economics and the application of economics for the greater good.”

Since 1982, the NABE award winner has been chosen “based on leadership in the profession and the application of economic principles and knowledge in the workplace and policy areas.”

In announcing this year’s recipient, NABE President Emily Kolinski Morris praised Chair Powell for his “exemplary leadership as chair of the Federal Reserve, especially navigating the complexities of monetary policy during challenging times. He has had a profound and lasting impact on both the US economy and the global financial system. Under his stewardship, the Federal Reserve has effectively managed unprecedented economic challenges … by taking a thoughtful and data-driven approach to monetary policy setting.”

“His decisive actions to support economic recovery, promote financial stability, and ensure a resilient financial system have demonstrated an unwavering commitment to US economic growth and well-being. The strength of his leadership has also earned him the respect of policymakers, economists, and business leaders worldwide. His work in the areas of monetary policy, financial regulation, and economic resilience absolutely aligns with the ideals of the Adam Smith Award. He has consistently applied foundational economic principles to address real-world challenges, upholding the Federal Reserve’s high standards of sound economic management and transparent policymaking in the process.”

Chair Powell On Current Economic Outlook And Monetary Policy Outlook

In accepting the Adam Smith Award, Chair Powell discussed his view of the current economic outlook and the monetary policy outlook. His comments repeated what he said at the September FOMC Press Conference.

He noted that the downside risks to employment have increased as payroll gains have “slowed sharply,” albeit as a result of the “decline in labor force growth due to lower immigration and labor force participation.”

Meanwhile, core PCE inflation was 2.9% y/y, “as rising core goods inflation has outpaced continued disinflation in housing services.”

He noted that due to the government shutdown, some important government data has been delayed. Based on the available data, Chair Powell stated, “The outlook for employment and inflation does not appear to have changed much since our September meeting four weeks ago.”

The only new piece of data that the Fed received prior to the shutdown was the GDP revision, which showed “that growth in economic activity may be on a somewhat firmer trajectory than expected.”

On September 25, the BLS released the third estimate for Second Quarter GDP, which was revised up by 0.5% to 3.8%.

This follows GDP’s first release of 3.0%, which was then revised up to 3.3% for the second release.

The revision, according to BLS, reflects a downturn in imports and an acceleration in consumer spending.

New Revelations On Monetary Policy

The title of Chair Powell’s acceptance speech was “Understanding the Fed’s Balance Sheet,” which is a topic he rarely spends a lot of time on yet has been a critical policy tool for how the Fed has conducted Monetary Policy over the past 17 years.

The Balance Sheet became important when the Fed switched their operating framework from a Scarce Reserves Regime to an Ample Reserves Regime. This change occurred when the Fed jumped in to prevent the economy and the banking system from collapsing during the Great Financial Crisis [GFC] of 2008. Their first step was to use their most effective Monetary Policy tool by cutting the Fed Funds Rate to almost zero. Once that tool was no longer available to them, they turned to more unconventional methods.

Using Open Market Operations, the Fed began a series of Large-Scale Asset Purchases, more commonly known as Quantitative Easing [QE], to try to stimulate the economy and add liquidity to the system. This resulted in a massive expansion of the Fed’s Balance Sheet, and also dramatically increased the amount of Bank Reserves in the system. The Fed’s Total Assets grew from $850 billion pre-GFC to a peak of almost $9 trillion.

The Fed made two significant changes to their operations during this time. First, they deviated from their traditional practice of only buying short-term Treasury Securities, and began buying Mortgage-Backed Securities [MBS]. This was done to improve the liquidity of banks by removing these illiquid assets, which were partially responsible for the onset of the GFC, from banks’ balance sheets. In doing so, they transferred the risk of owning these securities from the banks to their own balance sheet. In addition, the Fed purchased longer-term Treasury Securities as well, which also increased their exposure to interest rate risk.

Because the Fed was creating Bank Reserves to conduct QE, the second change they made was to begin paying Interest On Bank Reserves. This was a necessary step to provide an incentive for the banks to keep their reserves on deposit at the Fed.

In his expansive discussion, Chair Powell addressed these two changes.

First, with regard to the Fed’s security holdings, Chair Powell stated how the Fed wants to adjust the longer-run composition of their securities portfolio. Presently, the System Open Market Account (SOMA) is overweighted in long-term securities and underweighted in short-term securities, relative to the overall Treasury market. Powell stated that the Fed will be reviewing what the proper long-term maturity structure of their Treasury holdings should be.

.

In addition, Chair Powell emphasized that the Fed wants to return to their historical position of primarily owning Treasury securities.

As of October 9th, the Fed owned $4.2 trillion of Treasury Securities and $2.1 trillion of MBS, meaning to get an all-Treasury portfolio, the Fed needs to get rid of one-third of their SOMA holdings.

As for paying Interest On Bank Reserves, Chair Powell commented on how important this mechanism is in the execution of today’s Monetary Policy. Under the Ample Reserves Regime, there is sufficient liquidity to allow the Fed to control their policy rate through setting the Interest On Reserve Balances and the Overnight Reverse Repo Rate.

If the Fed’s ability to pay Interest On Reserves were eliminated, as some in Congress have suggested, the Fed would lose control over interest rates. “The stance of Monetary Policy would no longer be appropriately calibrated to economic conditions and would push the economy away from our employment and price stability goals. To restore rate control, large sales of securities over a short period of time would be needed to shrink our balance sheet and the quantity of reserves in the system. The volume and speed of these sales could strain Treasury market functioning and compromise financial stability. Market participants would need to absorb the sales of Treasury securities and agency MBS, which would put upward pressure on the entire yield curve, raising borrowing costs for the Treasury and the private sector. Even after that volatile and disruptive process, the banking system would be less resilient and more vulnerable to liquidity shocks.”

Powell believes that “our Ample Reserves Regime has proven remarkably effective for implementing Monetary Policy and supporting economic and financial stability.”

The Fed Is Coming To The End Of Quantitative Tightening

The Fed embarked on their Policy Normalization Plan in 2022 when they began raising the Fed Funds rate to battle the 40-year high in inflation.

A second part of Policy Normalization was to reduce the size of their Balance Sheet through maturity runoffs, which is more commonly known as Quantitative Tightening [QT].

The Fed has been executing QT for more than three and one-half years.

Over that period of time, the Fed’s Balance Sheet has contracted by $2.4 trillion, or by a decline of 26.5% from the peak. Total Assets now stand at $6.6 trillion, their lowest level in five and one-half years, since the beginning of the pandemic.

Balance Sheet reduction through maturity runoffs was the tool the Fed used, but their goal was to drain the liquidity that had been injected during QE in the form of Bank Reserves and Reverse Repurchase Agreements.

From its peak of $6.2 trillion, Total Liquidity has been reduced by $2.8 trillion, or by 45%. Current Total Liquidity now stands at $3.4 trillion, which is comprised of $3.0 trillion of Bank Reserves and $0.4 trillion of Reverse Repurchase Agreements.

As can be seen in the chart above, most of the reduction in liquidity has come from the decline in Reverse Repurchase Agreements (grey line.) This was by design. Bank Reserves have been relatively stable while Reverse Repurchase Agreements were shrinking. Now, though, that domestic Reverse Repos are essentially gone, further liquidity drain will come from Bank Reserves.

Almost since the Fed began QT, people have been asking when it will end. Chair Powell has always said that the Fed will stop QT when Bank Reserves are just above the level the Fed deems them to be ample.

He was a bit more revealing on Wednesday, when he said, “We may approach that point in coming months, and we are currently monitoring a wide range of indicators to inform this decision.”

Two of the indicators are the spread between the Secured Overnight Funding Rate [SOFR] and the Fed Funds Rate (blue line below) and the spread between the Tri-Party General Collateral Rate [TGCR] and the Interest On Reserve Balances (white line below.) Both are near the widest levels in a year.

In the Q&A after his presentation, Chair Powell said that although reserves are “still abundant,” with the slow pace of the runoff, we’re not so far away from being just above ample. He hedged a bit, though, and said, “We still have a ways to go.”

Chair Powell Addresses The Fed’s Operating Losses

Chair Powell did, however, for the first time, expand on the fact that because the Fed began paying Interest On Reserves, when the Fed hiked rates to fight inflation in 2022, the Fed began to lose money. The income they were earning on their fixed-rate Treasury securities and MBS remained unchanged, while the interest expense they had to pay on their variable-rate liabilities, namely Bank Reserves and Reverse Repurchase Agreements, rose significantly. The Fed has been losing money from operations for more than three years.

Powell tried to minimize this by stating that since the Fed began paying Interest On Reserves in 2008, the Fed has remitted to the Treasury more than $900 billion, and “having negative net income has no bearing on our ability to conduct monetary policy or meet our financial obligations.” While this may be accurate, it doesn’t tell the complete story.

For the first 15 years of that period, the Fed earned an average of $78 billion per year. By statute, these earnings are remitted to the Treasury each year. They are counted as a source of revenue and helped reduce the government’s fiscal deficit. However, for the past three years, the Fed has lost an average of -$75 billion per year. By Fed accounting, when the Fed experiences an operating loss, remittances to the Treasury are suspended. Instead, the loss is carried on the Fed’s balance sheet as a “Deferred Remittance To The Treasury.” Hence, the Treasury no longer receives the Fed’s operating earnings, and, as a result, this loss of revenue causes the government’s fiscal deficit to increase. Further, the Fed must create reserves to fund the deferred asset, and it must pay interest on the new reserves created. Ultimately, this cost must be paid by the taxpayers.

Not only that, even when the Fed starts having positive operating income, it must first be used to reduce the deferred asset before the Earnings Remittances To The Treasury can resume.

With a current Deferred Asset of -$245 billion, elimination of this account is not expected to occur before 2029. Hence, Treasury will be without the remittances from the Fed for a total of seven years or more.

Is The Fed Independent?

During the Q&A, Chair Powell was asked, “Given the scrutiny over the Fed’s policy decisions, what measures are you taking to ensure and to demonstrate the continued independence of the Fed in setting monetary policy?”

In his answer, Chair Powell reiterated, “We do our work as we always do. We make the best decisions to serve the American public by weighing the evolving economic conditions, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks to make good decisions. If we explain our decisions that are grounded in the data and our overall approach…people will be able to tell that we’re being independent.” (emphasis added)

Given this statement, it is surprising to me that CME FedWatch is predicting a 96% chance of a 25 basis point rate cut on October 29th.

The government shutdown has prevented the release of the October 3rd Unemployment report. With little progress on the battle in Congress to pass a continuing resolution to open the government, it is unlikely that the Fed will have any new federal government economic data on which to base their policy decision at the October FOMC meeting.

Chair Powell spoke about how there is private data to look at, particularly for employment, which is the Fed’s greater concern right now. He mentioned specifically state unemployment data and the private ADP payroll data. But these measures are just useful as supplemental inputs. They only enhance “the underlying government data, which is the gold standard…private data won’t be as effective as a main course…. We don’t expect we’ll be able to replace the data we won’t be getting.”

If this is the case, and Chair Powell said nothing has changed since the September FOMC meeting except a GDP “growth that is on a firmer trajectory than expected,” and official government data is not available, how can the Fed, which insists it is data dependent, make an important policy decision with no new information?

If they do cut rates without any new inputs, they will be deviating from their normal practice, which, to me, questions their independence, given the extreme political pressure to cut rates.

A further problem with regard to data that Chair Powell mentioned is the next employment report scheduled to be released on November 7th. If the government shutdown extends through the end of October (we’re already more than halfway there), BLS won’t have even done the work to collect and compile the data for next month’s releases. This creates an even bigger void.

What Chair Powell Didn’t Say

As a seasoned Financial Analyst, I have discovered that often you learn more about an institution by what they don’t say rather than what they do.

While Chair Powell touched on the Fed’s operating losses for the first time, he neglected to say how large they were (-$245 billion cumulatively, as discussed above) and that the Treasury would not receive remittances from the Fed for more than seven years.

Chair Powell also didn’t say that in addition to the operating losses, the Fed is carrying almost -$1 trillion in unrealized market losses on their security holdings in their SOMA portfolio. Fed accounting allows them to carry their Treasury and MBS securities at Amortized Cost, while only listing their Market Value in a footnote on their Balance Sheet. Since most of the securities were purchased when interest rates were much lower than they are today, the Fed has these unrealized losses.

While Chair Powell commented on how the Fed wants to hold only Treasury Securities in the long run, what he is implying, but didn’t say, is that buying MBS was a mistake.

He also didn’t say how the Fed should go about returning to owning only Treasury Securities.

The current method for the Fed to reduce their MBS holdings is through maturity runoffs. While under the Fed’s QT program they are allowing MBS to run off at up to $35 billion per month, the Fed has never hit that target in three and one-half years of QT. MBS are only running off at the rate of roughly $15 billion per month, which is primarily their scheduled amortization of principal. Due to the extremely high mortgage rates and homeowners being locked into their low-rate mortgages, there are virtually no prepayments. Consequently, at the current pace, the MBS portfolio will not be eliminated for 140 months, which is almost twelve years.

The faster way to reduce MBS is to sell them. This creates a new problem because the MBS portfolio is carrying a $360 billion unrealized loss. If the Fed were to sell their MBS, they would have to recognize that loss. They have chosen not to do this since they started QT, and with Chair Powell’s term expiring in May 2026, it is unlikely they will make the decision to sell MBS before he leaves.

Hence, while Chair Powell emphasized that the Fed wants to hold primarily Treasury Securities, he didn’t say that won’t occur until long after a new Fed Chair is appointed.

Chair Powell also emphasized how the Fed is overweighted in long-maturity Treasury Securities and underweighted in short-maturity Treasuries. While they will discuss how to rebalance their maturity structure, what he didn’t say was that if they were to do it through Treasury sales, they would confront the same problem as with MBS. The Fed’s Treasury Securities have an unrealized loss of almost $570 billion, and if they were to sell them, they would also have to realize losses.

While Chair Powell emphasized that the Fed’s losses did not affect the Fed’s ability to conduct Monetary Policy, what he did not say is that some of the Fed’s decisions with regard to structuring their SOMA portfolio as they desire for the long run were influenced by the Fed not wanting to recognize bigger losses.

Conclusion

As Jerome Powell comes to the end of his term as Fed Chair, he was named by the National Association for Business Economics as their 2025 Adam Smith Award recipient. Powell was recognized for his exemplary leadership of the Federal Reserve and for the outstanding job he has done managing Monetary Policy during extremely challenging times.

In discussing the economic outlook and monetary policy outlook, Chair Powell repeated what he said following the September FOMC meeting. The Fed is concerned about the downside risks to employment, and although inflation is elevated, they expect the impacts from tariffs to be a one-time increase. This was why the Fed cut rates by 25 basis points in September.

This was called a “Risk Management Cut” to prevent further deterioration in employment.

While the Fed is still concerned about the balance of risks between employment and inflation, the data-driven Powell stated that nothing has changed in the four weeks since the last FOMC meeting.

There was one new piece of government data, an upward revision to second-quarter GDP, which is now 3.8%, up from the originally reported 3.0%. This was due to surprising strength in consumer spending.

With the government shutdown, the all-important Unemployment Report that was supposed to be released on October 3 has been suspended. Chair Powell emphasized that private employment data, while helpful as a supplement, cannot replace the “gold standard” government data that is not being reported.

It is surprising to me that, with no new government data other than GDP showing strength in consumer spending, the Fed can make the rate cut that the market is expecting. CME FedWatch predicts there is a 97% chance rates will be reduced by 25 basis points on October 29.

Chair Powell didn’t indicate this, but he also did not try to dissuade the market from tempering their expectations.

Instead, Chair Powell focused on the mundane discussion of the Fed’s Balance Sheet. He hinted that QT will be ending in the coming months but hedged that there is still a ways to go.

The Fed is watching for signs of strain in the money markets so they will know when the current level of Abundant Reserves falls to just above the level of Ample Reserves.

data.title: ASML: A Winner In The AI Arms Race
data.description:
ASML (NASDAQ:ASML)(OTCPK:ASMLF) reported better-than-expected bottom line results for the third fiscal quarter on Wednesday, but missed on the top line. A continual boom in the semiconductor market, driven by AI-optimized chips, however, led ASML to report strong third quarter system bookings and robust underlying profitability in terms of gross margins and free cash flows.

The order situation for the firm’s lithography machines is very positive and fits into the broader narrative of growing CapEx spending in the semiconductor industry, with AI adoption being the main accelerant for expansion. Additionally, ASML confirmed its outlook for FY 2025 and continues to see strong CapEx momentum amid growing investments in AI-focused server infrastructure. While shares are not cheap, ASML still has revaluation upside as hyperscalers are set to pour billions of dollars into the Data Center market in the next several years.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of ASML a strong buy in my last work in August -- Fundamentals Are Fully Intact -- because I liked the firm’s strong momentum in net bookings for its lithography machines and because I believed that the market overreacted to the firm's growth warning at the time. Since ASML is already highly profitable, and generates a good amount of free cash flow on a recurring basis, I believe that the investment setup still favors long term investors with a desire to invest in the AI-driven technology market.

ASML Beat Q3 ’25 EPS Estimates

The equipment maker reported better-than-expected earnings results for its September quarter: ASML had GAAP earnings of €5.48 per share, beating the consensus estimate by €0.06 per share. The top line came in at €7.52B which was €210M below the average prediction.

Strong Third Quarter Bookings, High Gross Margins, Positive FCF

ASML is the world's leading equipment manufacturer in the semiconductor industry, producing high-performing EUV lithography machines that are needed for the high-precision task of manufacturing AI-capable chips. Strong demand for precision semiconductor manufacturing equipment, which includes ASML’s lithography system, has led the company to disclose strong net bookings for the third quarter: in Q3 ’25, the equipment maker generated total net bookings of €5.4B, showing 105% growth.

ASML recorded €7.5B in total revenue in the third quarter, showing a relatively moderate 1% growth rate year-over-year. However, as we will see later, the equipment maker confirmed its outlook for FY 2025 and guided for a strong fourth quarter performance... which is set to put the semiconductor enterprise on track to record double-digit top line growth for the full-year.

ASML’s gross margin remained solidly above 50% (at 51.6%) and fell within the firm’s previously communicated guidance range of 50-52%. The firm's financial accomplishments and margins for Q3 '25 are summarized in the following table.

ASML's gross margin trend, overall, is still very strong amid a continual AI boom: the firm consistently booked gross margins, on a GAAP level, above 50% in the last five quarters. In Q3 '25, ASML's gross margin increased 0.8 PP compared to the year-earlier period.

Confirmed Outlook For FY 2025

ASML expects to generate 15% top line growth, on a full-year basis, in FY 2025, meaning the semiconductor company confirmed its previous outlook of about €32.5B, at the midpoint. However, ASML guided for headwinds to its important market in China -- which represented 42% of total net system sales in Q3 ’25 -- as U.S. and Dutch export control restrictions on advanced lithography equipment are expected to have a negative impact on the firm’s business overseas.

ASML’s Valuation

ASML is the most expensive company in the semiconductor industry group, which is due to the fact that the firm operates in a highly capital-intensive industry… which acts as an entry barrier to the market. Shares of ASML are currently valued at a forward (FY 2026) price-to-earnings ratio of 33.2X, which makes the company even more expensive than Nvidia (NVDA).

Dominant AI tech plays like Nvidia and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), which are dominating their respective industries -- Nvidia dominated GPU chip design, while TSMC is the world's leading global fab with a market share of ~60% -- are trading at forward P/E ratios of 28.2X and 25.7X.

However, given ASML's unique position in the semiconductor value chain as a critical equipment manufacturer, I believe shares continue to have upside, despite the higher-than-average price tag.

In my opinion, shares of ASML could trade at ~35X forward earnings, under the assumption that the semiconductor company maintains its growth momentum and is capable of offsetting order weakness in China next year with sales growth in other regions. Analysts currently expect ASML to achieve $29.59 in earnings per share in FY 2026: applying a 35X earnings multiplier calculates to a fair value of ASML’s shares of approximately $1,035 per share, which may be a conservative value if CapEx spending trends hold up long term.

Risks With ASML

The biggest risk for ASML, as I see it, is a potential spending moderation in the hyperscaler market, potentially catalyzed by a recession. ASML said in its Q3 report that it expects slowing demand for its lithography systems from China next year, and last week's re-escalation of the tariff war between the U.S. and China is a major risk factor that investors need to keep in mind as well. In Q3 ’25, China represented 42% of net system sales, so a further deterioration in the booking situation could be a potential drag on the firm’s FY 2026 guidance, which is expected to be revealed in January 2026.

Final Thoughts

ASML reported a robust earnings scorecard for the third fiscal quarter amid continually robust spending momentum in the hyperscaler market, which ultimately underpins demand for AI chips. ASML remained widely profitable in the third quarter, in terms of both gross margins and free cash flow. The CapEx outlook for the Data Center industry remains widely favorable, with companies like OpenAI set to spend hundreds of billions of dollars alone, supporting an investment in ASML. The equipment maker is not a bargain from a valuation point of view, but I believe the fundamentals in the AI race support an investment in the semiconductor company.

data.title: The Impact Of U.S. Stock Buybacks: Theory Vs Practice
data.description:
Introduction

U.S. stock buybacks are on track for a record in 2025: estimated at $1.2 trillion according to JP Morgan, with expectations of surpassing $1.8 trillion in coming years [Bloomberg 2025]. This dwarfs new equity issuance through IPOs, secondary offerings and employee stock option grants, and represents 2 – 3% of U.S. equity market capitalization — 50% to 100% more than the annual payout from dividends.

Buybacks raise important questions. Foremost amongst them are whether, and how much, buybacks push up stock prices, and whether they create other distortions relevant to investors and public finances. This article explores these questions by drawing on economic theory and broadly held views of real-world investor behavior.

Central to our analysis is estimating the impact of buybacks assuming investors follow some combination of these three common approaches to asset allocation:

  • fixed-weight,
  • dynamic driven by expected cash-flow, and
  • dynamic driven by past returns (we’ll refer to this group as extrapolators or return-chasers).

Buybacks in theory

Franco Modigliani and Merton Miller laid down some of the cornerstones of modern corporate finance in the late 1950s. The most relevant of the Modigliani-Miller propositions for our purposes is their result that, given certain assumptions, payout policy through dividends and buybacks shouldn’t affect the enterprise value of a firm, which instead should be driven by the firm’s earnings and assets. They and their followers also showed that dividends and buybacks should be exactly equivalent.

These results only strictly hold under a strong set of assumptions, including frictionless and complete markets, rational investor behavior, and neutral tax treatment between dividends and capital gains. These assumptions are clearly not uniformly true in practice, as we’ll discuss below – nonetheless, Modigliani-Miller remains a useful starting point for expectations around the impact of dividends and buybacks.

Why executives love buybacks

In practice, executives favor buybacks for several reasons:

  • Flexibility: Executives believe that reducing dividends invites sharp market penalties compared to scaling back the pace of buybacks.
  • Tax efficiency: U.S. taxable investors can defer realizing gains from buybacks, often avoiding taxes altogether through deferral or stepped-up basis treatment. By contrast, dividends face an immediate top marginal rate of 24%. Buybacks are also more tax-efficient for non-US investors, who are subject to 15% – 30% withholding tax on dividends but no tax on capital gains.1
  • Share price support: Although rarely stated explicitly, executives are aware that buybacks can boost stock prices, aligning with incentive structures (i.e. increasing management compensation). In general, executive stock option awards would be worth less if companies returned funds to shareholders by paying higher dividends versus doing share buybacks.2

Market impact of buybacks

As described above, theory suggests that buybacks shouldn’t impact stock prices under the Modigliani-Miller assumptions because, while the count of shares is reduced, the firm also has less cash because of the buybacks and these effects exactly offset each other. In effect, it is assumed that shareholders value companies as a sum of their parts, and in particular that they value cash in the company or in their hands the same.

Empirically, however, there’s strongly suggestive evidence that buybacks do impact stock prices, and there are a number of models which can explain why this might be the case – all of which rely on investors not being fully rational. An important assumption of these models is that, to some extent, if the companies did not buy back their shares, then that cash would either be retained and productively invested by the companies3 or paid out as extra dividends which investors would either consume or keep in a higher allocation to fixed income, rather than reinvest in shares of the companies.

The “endowment effect” is a cognitive bias described in behavioral economics whereby people demand more to give up an owned item than they would be willing to pay to acquire it if they did not own it. In the buyback context, it suggests that investors would accept a lower fraction of equities in their portfolio if cash is returned to shareholders through dividends versus buybacks. Investors are likely to demand a higher price to sell their shares into a buyback than the price they’d be willing to pay to buy new shares with extra dividends.

Given these assumptions, the impact of buybacks on share prices arises from many investors using incomplete heuristics to determine how much of an asset they want to hold. For example, if they want to hold a constant proportional amount of some asset, and the available amount of the asset is reduced, the only way this can be accommodated is for the price of the asset to go up.

In 2017, we wrote a note “The Chemistry of 10% More” that discussed how to think about this impact:

In a world with two assets — cash and equities — how can investors in aggregate sell some of their holdings back to companies, and at the same time, keep the fraction of their portfolio allocated to equities constant? The price of equities must go up!

Consider an investor holding $50 in equities and $50 in cash who wants to maintain this 50/50 asset allocation. If he sells 3% of his equity holding, he’ll then have $51.50 in cash. He’ll need $51.50 in equity holdings to stay at a 50/50 allocation. His equity holdings will have to increase in value by 6% to be worth $53.00, so that after he sells $1.50 into the buyback, he’ll be left with the desired $51.50 of equities. So a 3% buyback will cause the whole market to rally by 6% if all investors want to keep a constant asset allocation of 50/50. The impact is greater if investors want to maintain a higher allocation to equities, and lower for lower desired allocations. If investors want to maintain 75% in equities, then buybacks of 3% of the market would require the market to go up 12%!

To estimate the price effect of large-scale buybacks, consider three types of “model” investors:

  1. Fixed-weight allocators (e.g. 50% equities / 50% other assets): To absorb 3% of stock repurchases, prices must rise ~6%.
  2. Expected cash-flow driven allocators: Adjust holdings based on expected return. For them, a ~1% price rise suffices, because they will want a proportionately lower allocation to equities as their long-term return declines due to the market price going up.4 These “fundamental” investors increase stock market price elasticity relative to the other two types of investors described above and below.
  3. Return-chasers: These investors extrapolate past returns into the future, increasing their demand for equities as prices rise. We can’t sensibly assume that all investors are return chasers, because then buybacks would set off a never-ending rising price spiral. However, if we assume that about one-third of investors are return chasers (and 1/3 each the other two types) then buybacks could push prices up ~7%.5

These model investors can exist in different combinations, operate over different time horizons, and have complex multi-year interactions with one another. While finance theory would suggest most investors are Type 2 “expected cash-flow allocators,” we believe that in reality the preponderance of investors are of Types 1 and 3. Fixed-weight asset allocators are probably the most common investor type, considering the trillions of dollars invested in target date funds, balanced funds, and in endowments, pension funds and foundations who very infrequently change their asset allocation.6 Putting this all together and simply looking at the range of results without fully considering all of the possible interactions, we can see how buybacks on the order of 3% could plausibly push up prices by 3 – 5%.

It is interesting to note that over the past ten years, the US Price-Earnings multiple has increased by 4% per annum. The 3% – 5% per annum potential impact of buybacks provides one explanation of this increase.7

Other estimates

One much-cited academic perspective comes from Gabaix and Koijen (2023), who argue that stock markets are “inelastic,” so relatively small shifts in demand can move prices dramatically. They estimate that every $1 of buybacks moves the market up by $5!8 Also see Barberis et al. (2015) and Haghani and White (2017) for earlier explorations of these effects.

For an alternative practitioner perspective, hedge fund portfolio managers we’ve spoken with often base their estimates of market impact on share of daily trading volume. A common rule of thumb they use is from Grinold and Kahn (1999), and discussed and connected with other models of price impact in Bouchaud (2022):

The parameter k is usually set to 1. Stock buybacks running at around $1.5 trillion per year represents about $6 billion of buying each day, which is close to 0.5% of daily equity trading volume. Stock market daily price volatility is about 1%, so the impact from this formula would be about 0.07% each day, or about 20% per annum!

We think both of these estimates of buyback price impact are too high, though they can be lessened in increasingly realistic models which incorporate factors such as a decay of the price effect over time. However, while the precise magnitude of impact is uncertain, the consensus is that buybacks can exert significant upward pressure on stock prices.

No free lunch

And yet, buybacks are not a panacea which can deliver permanently higher investor welfare. In our view, it’s surely right that the enterprise value of a stock is derived from its earnings and assets, and stock price dynamics which don’t increase earnings or assets can’t increase the ultimate benefit investors derive from stock ownership. In our note Unexceptional US Stock Market Earnings?, we discuss how companies can’t just “surf” GDP growth without also reinvesting earnings. Thus to the extent buybacks are a substitute for reinvestment rather than dividends, they may in fact be decreasing future earnings and long-term investor welfare.

One consequence of this earnings-centric perspective is that if buybacks raise stock prices, they must also lower forward-looking expected returns, thus resulting in neutral long-term investor welfare.

Potential distortions between individual stocks

Direct stockholders

Investors in individual stocks will tend to require a premium to the market price of the buyback stock that prevailed before the buyback was announced or executed, and indeed there is some evidence of buyback-heavy stocks having excess returns [Bloomberg 2025]. It is likely that the predictability of stock buybacks invites some front-running by astute trading firms and hedge funds. To the extent such trading firms make attractive risk-adjusted returns from buybacks, the likely losers are the stock-picking investors who sold too early. We suspect that over time, competition among trading firms will erode such profits, but not completely.

Investors who hold stocks directly and do not sell into buybacks find themselves increasingly overweight in buyback-active companies relative to benchmarks. Over time, this heightens portfolio idiosyncratic risk — often evading investors’ notice.

Index fund investors

Market-cap weighted index funds must rebalance as companies repurchase shares. This results in selling stock in companies doing buybacks and reallocating into companies not doing buybacks (or doing less buybacks). Note that market capitalization weighted index funds are behaving as theory would predict, willing to sell into buybacks at the prevailing market price. To the extent their activity has impact on relative stock prices, they will be pushing non-buyback stocks higher relative to buyback stocks, countering the impact of stock-pickers requiring a premium to sell their shares into buybacks.

Practitioners and researchers, such as Gabaix and Kroijen (2023), find that the price elasticity of individual stocks is greater than that of the the overall stock market. They explain this finding by noting that Apple and Microsoft are likely viewed as closer substitutes than the overall US stock market versus the total US bond market. We would add that the prevalence of index funds also can reduce price impact between stocks, at least in the case of buybacks.

Cyclical risk

Buybacks are highly pro-cyclical. They surge in bull markets and collapse in downturns—falling by over 50% in 2008 and 2020. This cyclicality can amplify both rallies and declines, exacerbating volatility. Looking ahead, large-scale equity issuance (e.g., from capital-intensive AI firms raising trillions for infrastructure) could have the reverse effect of today’s record buybacks, increasing supply and pressuring valuations.9

Tax and policy implications

Buybacks are also a tax policy issue:

  • U.S. taxable investors: Benefit from deferral and favorable treatment of capital gains versus dividends.
  • Foreign investors: Avoid 15–30% U.S. dividend withholding tax, meaning the Treasury collects nothing when capital is returned via buybacks.
  • Revenue impact: If one-third of U.S. equities are held by U.S. taxable and foreign investors, foregone revenues amount to $100–150 billion annually [Tax Policy Center 2024].

This helps explain why Congress imposed a 1% excise tax on buybacks in 2022, with proposals to raise it further. Policymakers argue this offsets revenue losses and curbs distortions created by large-scale repurchases.

Connecting the dots

Estimating the market impact of buybacks, or indeed any shifts in the supply and demand of stocks, is an area where theory and practice have not come to agreement. In researching their 2023 article, Gabaix and Koijen asked about 300 economists: “If a fund buys $1 billion worth of US equities, slowly over a quarter, how much does the aggregate market value of equities change?” The median answer they received was about 1/500th of their estimate of $5 billion and about 1/100th of ours. That’s a pretty big difference of opinion!

Our analysis of buybacks rested on the behavior of three types of investor, all of whom were assumed to be less than fully rational. Our discussion left out an important fourth investor type – fully rational investors who make their decisions based on predictions of what less-than-fully rational investors will do. Researchers have long believed that these rational investors will tend to destabilize markets by exacerbating the impact from exogenous supply and demand shocks, such as buybacks. For example, see DeLong, Shleifer, Summers and Waldman’s 1990 article, “Positive Feedback Investment Strategies and Destabilizing Rational Speculation.” Indeed, perhaps one explanation for the extraordinary financial success of quantitative trading firms, such as Jim Simon’s Renaissance Technologies Medallion fund, is their understanding of the behavior and interaction of the investor types described herein.

In practice, we believe there’s compelling evidence that buybacks:

  • Increase equity prices, possibly explaining part of the remarkable returns delivered by U.S. stocks over the past several decades in absolute terms, and also compared to the rest of the world. The resulting increase in prices, and consequently in price-earnings ratios, translates into lower long-term expected returns for investors.
  • Create distortions and frictions for index funds and direct investors.
  • Amplify cyclicality, boosting bull markets and deepening bear markets.
  • Erode U.S. tax revenues, benefiting both domestic taxable investors and foreign shareholders at the Treasury’s expense.

In sum, buybacks are not a neutral corporate finance choice but a force shaping market dynamics, investor welfare, and fiscal policy. Their rise since the 1990s may be one of the most under-appreciated drivers of U.S. equity market performance and warrants greater attention from academics, investors, and policymakers.

Further Reading and References

  • Barberis, N., Greenwood, R., Jin, L., and Shleifer, A. (2015). “X-CAPM: An extrapolative capital asset pricing model.” Journal of Financial Economics.
  • Bouchaud, JP. (2022). “The Inelastic Market Hypothesis: A Microstructural Interpretation.” arXiv.
  • Brockman, P., Lee, H.S. and Salas, J. (2025). “Share Repurchases and Investment Policies.” Wiley Online Library.
  • Brosy, T., & Rosenthal, S. (2024). “What is the U.S. Tax Advantage of Stock Buybacks over Dividends?” Tax Policy Center.
  • DeLong, B., Shleifer, A., Summers, L. and Waldmann, R. (1990a). “Noise Trader Risk in Financial Markets.” Journal of Political Economy.
  • DeLong, B., Shleifer, A., Summers, L., Waldmann, R. (1990b). “Positive feedback investment strategies and destabilizing rational speculation.” Journal of Finance.
  • Gabaix, X., & Koijen, R. (2021). “In Search of the Origins of Financial Fluctuations: The Inelastic Markets Hypothesis.” NBER and SSRN.
  • Grinold, R. and Kahn, R. (1994). Active Portfolio Management: A Quantitative Approach for Producing Superior Returns and Controlling Risk. McGraw Hill.
  • Haghani, V. and White, J. (2017). “The Chemistry of 10% More.” SSRN.
  • Haghani, V. and White, J. (2024). “Leverage It or Leave It? Making Sense of Turbo-charged ETFs.” SSRN.
  • Lou, D. (2012). “A Flow-Based Explanation for Return Predictability.” The Review of Financial Studies 25 (12).
  • Shleifer, A., and Summers, L. (1990). “The noise trader approach to finance.” The Journal of Economic Perspectives.
  • Stamm, L. (2025). “JP Morgan Sees Record U.S. Buybacks Jumping by Another $600 Billion.” Bloomberg.
  • “S&P500 Q2 Buybacks Press Release”. S&P Dow Jones.
  • Wigglesworth, R. (2025). “Lunch with the FT: Investor Jean-Philippe Bouchaud: ‘The whole bull run is because of an influx of money.’” Financial Times.

Notes

  1. Though U.S. company executives rarely cite this benefit to foreign investors as a reason for buybacks.
  2. Furthermore, that executive stock compensation is generally based on share price and not market capitalization creates incentives to return capital to shareholders by foregoing marginally positive net-present-value projects or through issuance of debt.
  3. A recent article by Brockman et al. (2025) suggests that buybacks do not lead to lower real investments compared to private and public companies who don’t do buybacks. However, we don’t find this conclusion compelling as firms that buy back their shares may be systematically different from those that don’t, and it doesn’t answer the critical question of what would investment have been without the repurchase.
  4. These investors are not acting fully rationally, though their heuristic is more complete than that of the fixed allocators. The Modigliani-Miller framework would suggest that these return- and risk-driven investors will not want to change their allocation to stocks after the return of capital through buybacks (or dividends) as their exposure to the risky earnings stream of the market remains constant without needing prices to move or to do any trades. The assumptions we used to come to the 1% impact from 3% buying is a starting allocation to equities of 25%, given a 3.5% expected real return and 1.5% risk-free rate. After a 3% buyback, they now have a 24.5% equity allocation, which is optimal for a lower risk premium of 1.96%. That risk premium is achieved by prices going up by 1.2%.
  5. Lots of assumptions need to be made around how return chasers form their updated expected returns based on past returns. In this case, we’re assuming that a 1% rise in stock prices leads return chasing investors to increase their forward looking expected excess return by about 8%, e.g. from 4% to 4.32%.
  6. And when they do, they seem to be as likely to be changing due to an extrapolation of historical returns as based on changes in future cash-flow based returns.
  7. From US stock market data provided by Robert Shiller The US multiple expansion over these years of heavy buybacks is even more remarkable considering that the real yield of US 10-year TIPS increased from around 0.5% at the start of the period to around 1.7% today.
  8. And also that the effect can be quite long-lasting unless buybacks are expected to be quickly reduced in the future.
  9. A recent JP Morgan report noted that the amount of debt tied to artificial intelligence has ballooned to $1.2 trillion, making it the largest segment in the investment-grade market. It is a little surprising that many of the companies returning cash to shareholders via buybacks are at the same time issuing large amounts of debt.

data.title: NIO And BYD Batteries Converging To Electrify The Future
data.description:
In China, the largest and fastest growing EV market, auto sales continue to slow, while the price war persists as local competition intensifies. Most recently, BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF / OTCPK:BYDDY) has introduced limited time discounts of up to 34% on select vehicles, while local upstart XPeng (XPEV) has reduced the sticker price on its upgraded line-up of flagship SUVs. Despite Beijing’s rebuke of the industry’s aggressive price cuts, competition continues as automakers look to entice buyers elsewhere. This includes Tesla (TSLA) and NIO’s (NYSE:NIO) rollout of brand-new, lower priced models, as well as their respective implementation of aggressive promotional offers – such as zero-interest financing and free charging vouchers – aimed at reducing prospective buyers’ total cost of ownership (“TCO”).

With the majority of local EV upstarts remaining unprofitable, many are racing to differentiate through technology as a way to boost demand, scale operations and overcome near-term margin deterioration. Common undertakings include competitive R&D in autonomous mobility developments and battery range improvements. Some EV makers have even ventured beyond core vehicle production, and into other areas across the broader EV technology value chain for differentiation – most notably Tesla, with its FSD and robotaxi ambitions.

Yet amid this diversification push, a more critical and imminently urgent component has been overlooked – namely, batteries, which remain the key enabler and biggest cost component of electrification. We believe this dynamic accordingly makes BYD and NIO two of the most differentiated players in China’s increasingly competitive EV industry. BYD has long been a key battery manufacturer and supplier alongside CATL (OTC:CTATF / OTC:CYATY). Meanwhile, NIO, is also converging into the same strategy. Although not a battery manufacturer, NIO has developed a proprietary battery swap technology that’s emerging as a key candidate for broad-based adoption alongside traditional charging.

Given intensifying competition and ongoing pricing pressure in the EV industry, batteries will remain a key differentiating moat. NIO and BYD’s converging path towards EV battery technology development are expected to be incrementally accretive for both companies’ fundamental prospects. It represents a critical lever and essential competitive advantage that will help preserve their margins and unlock upside potential that the market has yet to fully appreciate.

NIO’s Power Swap

NIO currently operates its own network of power intracultural, “NIO Power”. This consists of two critical pillars: 1) NIO Power Charger, and 2) NIO Power Swap.

Power Charger 4,0, specifically, is NIO’s most updated network of EV chargers. It has peak power of 640kW and maximum voltage of 1,000V, enabling ultra-fast charges. This compares to the performance of standard direct current (“DC”) fast chargers, which typically offer peak power of up to 400kW and could take as long as 30 minutes to recover 80% of battery range. To date, NIO has deployed more than 27,000 chargers across China to not only support its own brand of vehicles, but also those of its peers.

Where NIO truly differentiates, however, is its proprietary Power Swap network. Specifically, NIO has designed its vehicle line-up to be compatible with its proprietary battery swap technology. All vehicles across NIO, ONVO, and Firefly are capable of having their batteries swapped out at the Power Swap station for a fully charged one within three minutes. It’s also highly convenient – the cars are configured to automatically back themselves into the swap stations, and the swaps are performed without driver intervention. The Power Swap stations are also equipped with technology to perform “health checks” on both the battery and electric drive system of the car. This helps to ensure NIO vehicles are always equipped with optimal performance. To date, NIO has deployed more than 3,542 Power Swap stations across the world, with more than 1,000 of them stationed alongside major China highways to support buyers’ daily commute with ease. The network has also completed more than 84 million swaps to date, signaling growing user adoption of the nascent technology.

BaaS Pricing

NIO’s “battery-as-a-service” – or “BaaS” – model complements adoption of its swap technology. NIO vehicle buyers can opt for meaningful MSRP rebates if they choose to sign onto NIO’s BaaS battery subscription instead of buying the battery outright.

  • NIO: The core brand vehicle line-up shaves up to RMB 70,000 off their respective MSRPs, with an additional promotion of RMB 38,000 right now. BaaS rental starts at RMB 728 per month for the 75kW battery pack, and rises to RMB 1,128 per month for the 100kW battery pack, offering drivers flexibility based on their commuting needs.
  • ONVO: The ONVO L60 and L90 with BaaS is RMB 57,000 and RMB 106,000 cheaper, respectively, than their sticker prices. Current L60 purchases with BaaS also come with 144 free swap vouchers.
  • Firefly: The Firefly with BaaS is currently RMB 40,000 cheaper than its standard MSRP.

In addition to upfront pricing rebates and promotions, another key appeal of BaaS for prospective buyers is that the program reduces TCO by ensuring upgradability of the battery. Specifically, the battery is currently the largest cost component of an EV. They typically last 12 to 15 years under standard conditions, with their useful life potentially reduced to eight years with high use under extreme conditions. Similar to common device batteries, a common shortfall in EV batteries is that their charge retention is poised to deteriorate over time with use. And replacements could cost as high as $16,000, depending on the power capacity (average cost of an EV battery is currently about $111 per kW). Yet NIO’s BaaS program mitigates this headache. As mentioned earlier, a battery health check is performed during every swap to ensure NIO vehicles are only fitted with performing units, which effectively reduces concerns over high repair and maintenance costs.

Scalability Through Mass Market Penetration

To sustain network economics, NIO has been furthering the expansion of Power Swap into rural regions to complement its newly introduced sub-brands in penetrating concentrated mass market opportunities. Through the “Power Up Counties” initiative, the company aims to install Power Swap coverage across more than 2,300 counties in 27 major provincial administration regions in China by the end of the year. expansion will continue to the remaining regions through 2026.

The key rationale is to deepen NIO’s penetration into concentrated electrification opportunities across China’s price-sensitive mass market, which will help scale its offerings. As discussed in our previous coverage on the stock, only about a quarter of China’s cities currently exceed the national average EV penetration rate of 42%. The remaining population of 800 million concentrated in less affluent tier 3-5 regions have yet to make the switch to electric. The vital role of setting up Power Swap stations across rural counties is further corroborated by Nio’s sales performance in equipped regions. Specifically, NIO management has recently disclosed that sales volumes in Hubei Province and Anhui Province have surged to above average levels after expansion of its Power Swap coverage beyond the big cities and into county locations. This provides validation that availability drives adoption, and helps to ensure scalability of the Power Swap network alongside NIO’s core EV sales.

Third-Party Partnership Momentum

NIO has also been active in forging critical third-party partnerships to step-up adoption and deployment of its proprietary battery swap technology in recent years:

  • Geely (2023): Both companies agreed to cooperate on the joint development of battery swapping technology standards for private and commercial vehicles, and share infrastructure build-out. Geely is currently the second largest NEV brand in China after BYD with a market share of 12.5%. This underscores potential for further battery swap technology adoption in the long-term, which would be beneficial to NIO.
  • Changan (2023): Both companies agreed to cooperate on the joint development of swap-compatible vehicles, and build and share their battery swap networks. Changan is currently one of the top four automobile groups in China, which makes their partnership a reinforcement to the future of NIO Power Swap adoption.
  • Chery: Chery’s EV sub-brand Exeed is expected to become the first non-NIO vehicle to adopt and deploy NIO’s proprietary battery swap technology. Exeed is expected to move forward with two battery swap-compatible modes – including one SUV and a sedan – later this year.
  • CATL: Earlier in March, NIO and CATL – the world’s largest EV battery maker – joined hands to advance the development and adoption of battery swap technology for passenger EVs. This is critical to reinforcing NIO’s Power Swap prospects of becoming an industry standard long-term. Under the partnership, CATL is contributing up to RMB 2.5 billion into NIO Power. Both companies are committed to sharing their battery swap networks, as well as the R&D of related technology advancements. CATL’s own “Choco-Swap” battery swap stations and related technical standard will also be implemented in Firefly’s powertrain.

There have also been additional government tailwinds supportive of NIO’s Power Swap build-out and adoption. Shanghai, for example, is currently subsidizing up to 40% of construction costs for general-purpose battery swap stations; those that are brand-exclusive will be eligible for a reduced subsidy of 20%. The city’s cab drivers are also eligible for a subsidy of up to RMB 200 per month if they operate a battery swap-compatible vehicle, further fostering adoption of the technology long-term. And NIO stands to be a direct beneficiary of emerging policy support, given its first-mover advantage in battery swap technology.

Financial and Strategic Implications

Taken together, NIO’s battery swap technology developments represent a key lever for the company in driving long-term margin accretion alongside core EV sales. And the economics of this nascent business is showing gradual improvements. Management currently estimates that it takes about 60 swaps per station per day for it to breakeven. About 20% of NIO’s swap stations – primarily those located in larger cities like Shanghai – are already profitable. Meanwhile, on average, a NIO swap station currently facilitates about 30 swaps per day.

As a result, the notable partnerships forged will be key to bolstering long-term utilization at NIO’s expanding Power Swap network and contributing further to the business’ improving unit economics. The partnerships are also expected to drive consumer awareness, and reinforce a virtuous adoption cycle for the nascent technology. Specifically, more than 70% of NIO vehicle buyers already choose the BaaS purchase option. Meanwhile, about 60% of ONVO users have cited that the brand’s “capability of doing power swap is the top purchasing reason for them”.

Over the longer-term, NIO is also positioned to monetize its proprietary battery swap technology through the sale of third-party licenses – a high margin revenue stream. With greater deployment of swap-compatible powertrains over time, their scale efficiency is poised to improve as well, thus reducing vehicle bill of materials (“BOM”) and lifting vehicle gross margins further.

This trajectory aligns critically with shareholders’ interest. Sustained improvement in NIO’s BaaS and Power Swap unit economics will be key to driving NIO closer towards an earnings inflection and operating self-sufficiency – a key focus area for investors as the company remains challenged by persistent losses.

BYD’S Blade Battery and Super-E Platform

Unlike NIO, BYD does both the design and manufacturing of its own batteries. By leveraging deep vertical integration from the raw-material processing stage to cell manufacturing and assembly, BYD benefits from both cost leadership and technological control in its electrification strategy.

It’s currently the second largest EV battery supplier in the world behind CATL, and holds an 18% market share. In addition to supplying its own fleet, BYD also sells EV batteries – particularly, its proprietary “Blade” LFP batteries – to peers including Tesla and NIO. Specifically, BYD currently supplies NIO’s ONVO sub-brand with its Blade batteries. However, unlike its peer CATL, BYD has yet to forge any partnerships with NIO to standardize and deploy battery swap technology in the long-run.

Super-E Platform: Enabling ICE Parity

In the latest developments, BYD has introduced its all-new “Super-E Platform” battery architecture. The Super-E Platform combines “flash-charging batteries, a 30,000 RPM motor, and new silicon carbide power chips”, which collectively is capable of enabling 249 miles of range within five minutes of charge time. This is largely thanks to the platform’s peak charging speed of up to 1,000 kW, and compares against NIO Power Charger 4.0’s 640kW and the average 400kW delivered by standard DC fast chargers.

The innovation represents a huge step forward in assuaging range anxiety – a key roadblock to EV adoption besides affordability concerns. However, the power-intensive platform is unlikely compatible with the majority of existing charging infrastructure available in China at this time. As a result, only BYD’s upcoming Han L sedan and Tang L SUVs will launch with powertrains equipped with the Super-E Platform, while BYD aims to build over 4,000 ultra-fast chargers – or “Megawatt Flash Charging Stations” – across China to support incremental rollout of the new battery technology.

Financial Implications of Integration

Financially, BYD’s battery operations represent an accretive moat to its current leadership in scaling EV sales across China and globally. Due to the scale of its battery operations, which spans beyond EV application, BYD benefits from a cost advantage across “every stage of production” from raw material sourcing to the end product. This has likely been a key factor to BYD’s ability in keeping its vehicle line-up affordable for consumers without sacrificing profitability, while many of its local rivals that remain challenged by persistent losses.

As mentioned earlier, BYD’s battery operations is a key diversification for its earnings beyond core vehicle sales. Not only does being a third-party supplier drive further margin accretion, but it also permits BYD in indirect revenue-sharing with competition as electrification accelerates. Looking ahead, the mass deployment of the Super-E Platform could further BYD’s edge, as the technology enables charge time parity with the refueling of traditional ICE vehicles.

Still, insufficient and non-compliant grid capacity remains a key roadblock to the deployment and adoption of BYD’s Super-E Platform. Hence, scaling the technology and fostering mass market adoption will require significant time and capital. Until then, adoption is expected to be gradual, as the nascent technology’s rollout remains selective and geographically constrained. This is consistent with BYD’s contained go-to-market strategy for its Super-E Platform by limiting initial deployment to the Han L sedan and Tang L SUV within China only.

Opportunity for Convergence

Taken together, it’s evident that both NIO and BYD are converging in their focus on honing their respective battery strategies, which is a critical competitive advantage in the broader EV industry. More importantly, their respective battery strategies are also looking increasingly complementary, which poses a synergistic opportunity overtime. Since BYD is already a key supplier for NIO’s ONVO sub-brand, there is potential for deeper collaboration long-term, similar to NIO’s recent partnership with CATL in standardizing the future rollout of battery swap technology to third-parties.

Specifically, NIO’s three-minute battery swap and BYD’s five-minute ultra-fast charging are both designed to increase user experience parity with traditional ICE vehicles and alleviate range anxiety. Instead of substitutes, we see opportunity for the two technologies to function as complementary solutions for accelerating EV adoption.

As mentioned earlier, grid limitations remain the key roadblock to the rollout of BYD’s Super-E Platform. This can be compensated by NIO’s battery swap technology and Power Swap stations. For instance, if a BYD Megawatt Flash Charging Station faces downtime, a swap-compatible car can opt for a three-minute change-out at NIO Power Swap instead. Conversely, during NIO Power Swap downtime, vehicles could opt for the five-minute Megawatt Flash Charger; Power Swap can also leverage Megawatt Flash Charging to improve uptime and turnover. This highlights how the convergence of both companies’ battery developments can help solve range anxiety and unlock the next phase of scaling EV deployment through differentiation.

We think this integration is opportunistic and likely will be imminent. It’s likely only a matter of time until BYD starts to license its Super-E Platform to its Blade battery customers in order to optimize the project’s ROI structure. This will be a key benefit to the broader EV industry – especially NIO, given rapid charging can complement its proprietary three-minute swap technology.

Paring fast-charging and battery swap systems could also reduce the need for large battery packs. As mentioned earlier, the battery remains the largest cost component of an EV, averaging $111 per kW. If the combination of BYD’s Super-E Platform and Megawatt Flash Charging technologies with NIO’s battery swap system can alleviate range anxiety, then the need for large battery packs in EVs will also be reduced. This will be key to further lowering EV costs and improving affordability, which is critical for penetrating underserved opportunities in China’s price-sensitive mass market.

Final Thoughts: Battery Convergence is an Underappreciated Re-Rate Catalyst

It’s evident that battery technology is the foundational layer to both NIO and BYD’s long-term competitiveness.

Admittedly, BYD’s battery moat is on a much more mature footing than NIO’s. However, scaling nascent developments like the Super-E Platform, though innovative and directly addresses range anxiety, remains challenged due to limited charging infrastructure compatibility and insufficient grid capacity.

Meanwhile, NIO’s battery swap ecosystem, though newer, could solve constraints in traditional charging and redefine the convenience of EV ownership to further adoption. More importantly, NIO’s first-mover advantage in battery swap technology also enables multiple monetization levers over the longer-term. This spans BaaS subscriptions, third-party licensing sales, and infrastructure partnerships, which could collectively unlock a higher margin profile, and drive incremental accretion with earnings from its core EV sales.

Yet despite their competitive battery capabilities, both companies’ stocks have been trading near multi-year lows. Although the broader Chinese EV industry remains blighted by intensifying competition and a persistent price war, the structural synergy from local battery developments shouldn’t be overlooked.

As discussed in the foregoing analysis, we see room for convergence, which could unlock incremental financial synergies to both companies, while also fostering broader EV adoption. The dual integration of BYD’s Super-E Platform and NIO’s battery swap technology could accelerate utilization, effectively driving scale and lowering operating costs. These savings could, in turn, be passed on to lower consumer TCO and further encourage EV adoption, unlocking a virtuous positive cycle that improves scalability and earnings for both companies. This could drive a valuation uplift to both stocks, as corroborated by the meaningfully higher multiples awarded to battery pureplays like CATL and LG Energy Solution. Even if they do not collaborate, we believe both companies’ continued R&D focus on their respective battery ecosystems remain an underappreciated optionality capable of driving sustained margin expansion in the longer-term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alphabet: The Proof That AI Is Finally Paying Off
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ:GOOGL, NASDAQ:GOOG) continues to prove why it’s one of the most reliable performers in the tech sector. Since my last analysis, the stock has climbed ~9%, outpacing the broad market’s 3.45% return.

Alphabet has consistently beaten market estimates quarter after quarter. In Q2 ‘25, Alphabet’s diluted non-GAAP EPS came in at $2.31, outpacing estimates by 5.09%.
