This also marks the 10th consecutive quarter of earnings beats. On the revenue side, the company brought in $96.43 billion, which also exceeded estimates by 2.54%. And over the past 10 quarters, Alphabet has outpaced revenue expectations nine times.

These figures tell a story about how well the business operates. With this, and all the other reasons outlined in this analysis, my Strong Buy rating for Alphabet stays in place.

Q2 '25 Core Financials (Snapshot)

Here’s a glance at what happened during Q2 ‘25. Revenue rose ~14% to $96.42 compared to the same period last year. Companies of this size often struggle to maintain double-digit growth as the law of large numbers takes effect. So Alphabet's 14% revenue growth, given the company’s sheer size, is already impressive.

Gross profit rose 16.5% to $57.39 billion, and this tells us about the company’s pricing power and operational efficiency, which are expected to translate into better profitability down the line. Meanwhile, Operating income increased 14% to $31.27 billion, giving Alphabet an operating margin of 32.43%. In practical terms, it means that for every dollar of revenue generated, Alphabet retains $0.32 as operating profit before interest and taxes. Again, for a company of this size, it’s exceptional.

As a result, Alphabet’s bottom-line figures for the quarter rose 19.4% to ~$28.2 billion, translating to a basic EPS of $2.33 - notably higher compared to $1.91 in the same period last year. Alphabet also ended the quarter with over $21 billion in cash and cash equivalents, which means ample cash to invest in moonshot projects or return money to shareholders through dividends or share repurchase programs.

GOOGL Stock Profile

So, where does GOOGL stock stand today? At the time of this publication, the stock trades at $251.03 per share. Since the beginning of the year, the stock has gained nearly 33%, which is impressive considering all the macroeconomic headwinds we experienced during Q1 ‘25. On a 1-YR basis, the stock has gained ~52%, and nearly 233% on a 5-YR scale.

It wasn’t too long ago when skepticism was rampant, yet Alphabet has consistently created value for its shareholders.

On the valuation front, GOOGL stock trades at FWD P/E GAAP of 24.69x, which is approximately 33% higher than the sector median of 18.53x. Personally, I think the premium makes sense considering what you’re getting.

Apart from its solid financial track record, the company continues to dominate the search market despite all the regulatory hurdles and is one of the largest cloud computing providers, as well as a top performer in the AI development space, which is arguably the most lucrative market of this decade.

Also, compared to other mega-cap tech companies like Tesla (TSLA), Alphabet’s valuation actually appears more reasonable. As a result, I expect Alphabet’s valuation will continue to climb as the market recognizes the company’s concrete fundamentals and current position in the AI race.

What To Look For In Q3 '25?

There are indeed some key factors that I’ll closely monitor in Alphabet’s upcoming earnings on October 29, 2025, as these will help shape Alphabet’s stock performance in the following quarters and years.

Google Cloud

First is Google Cloud’s growth trajectory, because it's one of the Alphabet’s most important growth drivers for the company. In Q2, the segment’s revenue rose 32% to $13.6 billion. At this pace, Google Cloud runs at an annual run rate exceeding $54 billion, which is crucial considering there’s still a lot of runway in terms of cloud computing revenue.

Businesses worldwide are still migrating their office servers, apps, databases, etc., to the cloud while also incorporating AI capabilities. As a result, Alphabet’s cloud margin expanded 188% to 20.7% from just around 11% in the same period last year. Initially, Alphabet’s cloud business could barely break even, since they had to invest heavily in infrastructure and win customers; however, Google Cloud is now delivering impressive margins.

The question for Q3 ‘25 is whether the segment can continue growing over 30% year over year while retaining margin levels exceeding 20%. Personally, I think it can, considering that supply-demand conditions are expected to remain tight into 2026. According to Google Cloud CEO Thomas Kurian:

So our remaining performance obligation or backlog is sometimes referred to, is now at $106 billion. It is growing faster than our revenue. More than 50% of it will convert to revenue over the next 2 years.

This $106 billion in backlog is the amount that the customer has already committed to spending but hasn’t billed for yet. If at least 50% of this backlog truly converts into revenue, that would be a good indicator of where the company’s growth is heading.

The backlog itself rose 38% compared to the same period last year, while the customer count on Google Cloud Platform increased 28% quarter-over-quarter. If these figures continue to grow in the following quarters, it would be a good sign for the segment. And don’t forget, these customers aren’t the smaller mom & pops; rather, they are large enterprise deals that typically come with multi-year commitments.

Bottom line: Switching to a different provider would be complicated and expensive, making cloud expansion a sticky stream of revenue for Alphabet.

Operating Margins

Although Google Cloud’s margins are expanding, Alphabet is also facing pressure from its investments in AI infrastructure. During Q2 ‘25, depreciation grew 35% to $5 billion, and management expects that growth rate to accelerate in Q3 ‘25. Sundar Pichai, Alphabet's CEO, stated:

In the second quarter, depreciation increased $1.3 billion year-over-year to $5 billion, reflecting a growth rate of 35%. Given the recent increase in CapEx investments, we expect the growth rate in depreciation to accelerate further in Q3.

For the unfamiliar, depreciation accounts for CapEx that devalues over time. Just as your car has a useful economic life of ~20 years, so do servers. Only that when a company builds data centers and purchases equipment like switching gear, servers, cooling equipment, etc., it can’t expense all that spending right off the bat. Instead, the investments are spread over the useful life of these assets. So, as Alphabet ramps up AI spending, depreciation charges also increase- and this puts pressure on operating margins.

On the topic of CapEx, the company also raised its guidance for 2025 to $85 billion from the initial $75 billion target, with expected further increases in the following years. In perspective, $85 billion is larger than the total annual revenue of many Fortune 500 companies. The fact that Alphabet is committing this much to AI means two things: there’s enormous potential in AI, and the competition is so intense that staying competitive requires significant investments.

Naturally, as guided by management, depreciation rates might accelerate in Q3 ‘25, but I think it’ll pay off. I mean, Alphabet has a solid track record of monetizing its previous infrastructure buildouts. That said, execution risks are also real, particularly with its AI infrastructure investments. For this, investors should closely watch whether these investments will pay dividends as Alphabet expects.

Advertising

In Q2 ‘25, Alphabet also experienced tailwinds in its advertising business. The segment’s revenue increased 10% to $71.3 billion compared to the same period last year. However, the Q3 ‘25 numbers may not be as strong as those in Q3 ‘24, as they were driven by the strong U.S. election advertising spending in 2024.

It’s important to be aware of these headwinds, but I don’t think it should concern long-term investors, as these are just near-term consolidations driven by timing issues, not actual structural problems that would erode Alphabet’s advertising market share.

AI Monetization

Q3 ‘25 and the following quarter will help show whether Alphabet can continue converting its AI investments into an actual revenue stream. So far, the progress has been promising.

As per industry reports, Gemini token processing volume has reached 980 trillion monthly tokens in Q2 ‘25, which is double that of May 2025. These tokens are units that AI models process. So, more token processing means more usage and demand for Alphabet’s AI, which is Gemini. The fact that it doubled in just a few months indicates widespread adoption of enterprises and consumers of these digital tools. Indeed, there are currently more than 85,000 enterprises using Gemini, which is up 35x compared to the same period last year in terms of usage.

That said, the clearest sign of monetization comes from AI Overviews in search. You know, those AI summaries you get from Google queries? That same feature is now being used by more than 2 billion users in over 200 countries. Now, management confirmed that AI overview ads monetize at about the same rate as traditional Google Search ads, and this validates the entire strategy.

Many investors are also worried that AI summaries would undermine the advertising model as they reduce the clicks to the advertiser’s website, but so far, that hasn’t happened.

Personally, I think Q3 ‘25 will be about proving that AI monetization is real. I mean, Q2 '25 gave us promising signs, but one quarter doesn’t make a trend. If Alphabet can show progress on both search monetization and cloud revenue, that will significantly strengthen the market sentiment.

What Do The Experts Say About Alphabet?

A consensus among 67 analysts rates Alphabet a “Buy,” a sentiment that has held steady for the past 3 years. The consistency alone is notable, as analyst ratings frequently change based on quarterly beats or market conditions. But Alphabet has maintained positive opinions despite all the headwinds and the current market cycle. That said, I wouldn’t use analyst ratings as the only reason to invest in the stock- though it does provide a useful data point about what Wall Street says, and a starting point for a much deeper analysis.

Verdict

Alphabet’s Q3 ‘25 earnings report on October 29th will be important to project the company’s long-term growth potential. Recent headwinds from advertising growth and depreciation expense are real, but I think they’re quite manageable given Alphabet’s track record and long-term potential. The company generates significant amounts of cash and dominates multiple segments. For those seeking exposure to the tech sector through a time-tested company, Alphabet is a promising option. As mentioned above, my Strong Buy rating for Alphabet remains in place for all the reasons stated.

data.title: Gemini Space Station: Major Growth Catalysts In Place To Drive Growth
data.description:
Investment Summary

My recommendation for Gemini Space Station (NASDAQ:GEMI) is a buy rating, as I believe the market is not appreciating its growth potential. The Gemini Credit Card is scaling faster than expected, creating a powerful funnel for recurring users, while the Nasdaq partnership opens a high-margin institutional channel. Together, these catalysts diversify revenue, improve earnings quality, and support a clear growth path ahead.

Business Overview

At its core, GEMI runs a global crypto exchange where both retail investors and large institutions can trade and store digital assets. GEMI is a global crypto exchange founded in 2014. The business provides a platform for both retail and institutional clients to buy, sell, and store crypto assets. Beyond the core exchange, which accounted for ~69% of 2024 net revenue, GEMI offerings include staking, custody services, tokenized stocks, and a rapidly expanding credit card business that provides crypto rewards. As of July 2025, GEMI served ~549,000 monthly transacting users [MTUs] and 10,000 institutions across >60 countries.

The Underestimated Growth Potential

In my opinion, the market is probably overlooking (if not, the share price wouldn’t be at this level) what could become GEMI’s most powerful growth driver—the Gemini Credit Card. This is a lot more than just a new product line; I see this as a solid customer acquisition funnel that is scaling rapidly and showing clear product-market fit. For perspective, the August 2025 launch of the co-branded Ripple card drove more than 30,000 new sign-ups in a single month, and this is more than double the prior month’s figure (per S-1 filings). This monthly sequential growth strength shows us that consumers want the product.

The implication of this ramp-up in adoption has a much bigger impact than just being an incremental revenue stream. Management has indicated that cardholders have higher lifetime value than non-cardholders, and I think this is the key that readers should really comprehend. The way I see it, the card effectively acts as the “bridge” to the core exchange, allowing users to earn rewards in crypto through their daily spending. This creates natural cross-sell opportunities while making users more engaged within the platform.

The easy way to think about this is to see this model as something similar to earning frequent flyer miles. Every time a customer buys coffee or groceries, they accumulate crypto rewards that automatically sit in their Gemini account. Those rewards form a small starter portfolio at zero cost. Once users have that, the next logical move is to make it grow. From here, two straightforward cross-sell opportunities emerge: staking and trading.

Starting with staking, GEMI can encourage users to put their earned crypto to work instead of letting it idle. Staking, in simple terms, means locking up crypto to help secure the network and receiving more tokens in return. Basically, this yields on your assets. For first-time users, this is a low-risk, intuitive way to learn about earning passive income on digital assets. Once they see their rewards balance compounding, confidence builds, and they will likely move to trading. For trading. They might convert a portion of their bitcoin rewards into ether, or vice versa, and from there they become more active traders.

Hence, what this illustrates is that the Gemini card is far more than a payments product; it effectively converts casual spenders into potential long-term exchange users. Because customers are initially playing with “house money,” the friction to engage is minimal, and the incentive to stay involved is strong. Over time, I believe this should help smooth the cyclicality of trading activity and expand GEMI’s recurring revenue base.

Gemini’s user growth story also reflects consumers' demand for it. The way I benchmark this is by comparing it to similar nascent-fast-growth platforms like Coinbase (COIN) and Robinhood (HOOD). Since Q1 2025, GEMI MTUs have grown 17.8%, outpacing both COIN and HOOD. Recent metrics even show accelerating traction. MTUs climbed from ~523,000 at the end of Q2 2025 to ~549,000 by the end of July, which implies a quarterly sequential add growth of ~15% (if we assume the same traction for the remaining months in Q3), or ~60% run-rate growth.

Regulatory Hurdles

Another underappreciated aspect, I believe, is the significance of GEMI’s decision to establish Gemini Moonbase in Florida as its primary US exchange. This is beyond a normal administrative move; I think this is actually a move to unlock further growth, one that has been constrained by New York’s tighter regulatory framework. In the past, the New York trust charter served as a badge of compliance, and that gave GEMI the credibility it needed. However, this badge is now increasingly limiting GEMI product velocity and ability to stay competitive.

For those that have followed the crypto world, things change very fast, and participants in the ecosystem want newness to stay engaged, and hence, with this structure in Florida, GEMI can now better compete on product breadth, which is an important differentiator in retail crypto. The ability to list a wider range of assets, and this is particularly for smaller, niche tokens that drive social media buzz (like Fartcoin, which had a market cap of $2 billion at one point). This is because, for retail investors, the best way to convert them is through these tokens (everyone talks about it and makes money from it, so they want a piece of it as well). The demand pull is natural. When new investors look for a specific altcoin, they naturally gravitate toward the platform with the broadest selection. Therefore, by operating under a more flexible regulatory regime, GEMI can now fully capitalize on this trend.

The Nasdaq Partnership

The partnership with Nasdaq is a really big game-changer for GEMI. Not only did it provide GEMI with a validation, but it also gave it a major new distribution channel. On validation, I see Nasdaq’s agreement to purchase $50 million of GEMI stock in a private placement represents a strong vote of confidence, and this matters for investors because Nasdaq is one of the most established and widely recognized exchanges in traditional finance. Simply put, this showed the market that GEMI “passes” the credibility and regulatory due diligence of Nasdaq.

But the real game-changing element in this deal is the commercial agreement. Nasdaq will work with GEMI to enable its own corporate clients (including listed companies and member firms) to custody and stake digital assets. This is incredibly huge because it effectively transforms Nasdaq into a direct sales channel for the GEMI institutional platform, which gives it access to a huge market of corporate treasuries (this figure is dated, but it shows the size). The financial impact is a stream of high-margin, recurring institutional revenue, and this draws back to my point above that more recurring revenue will smooth out the cyclicality of GEMI’s trading revenue.

Valuation

I know that the crypto space is a very volatile space, so projecting revenue growth may be hard. Hence, my assumptions lean conservative, expecting revenue growth to slow down to 20% over the next 4 years. For reference, when COIN had a revenue base of ~$533 million in FY19, it went on to grow the business to a size of $7 billion as of the last twelve months. Therefore, I think my assumptions may be too conservative, especially with the recurring revenue growth potential (from Gemini Card and Nasdaq partnership). But suppose GEMI performs as I expected; it should trade at 10.5x forward revenue, basically the same multiple that COIN trades today with a high-teens revenue growth outlook. Deducting net debt and dividing by the share count gives me a share price target of $42.

Risk

Despite the growth story, there are significant risks to consider. The crypto space is a very competitive industry, facing pressure from unregulated offshore exchanges that have lower compliance costs, as well as from traditional financial firms that are increasingly entering the crypto space (like Interactive Brokerage offering users the choice to invest in Bitcoin). While GEMI has gotten the badge of recognition from large industry heavyweights, the uncertain regulatory landscape will keep being a headwind. Any adverse legal actions or new rules could force GEMI to alter its products or halt operations in key jurisdictions.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for GEMI. The combination of accelerating card adoption, a larger institutional footprint through Nasdaq, and a more flexible regulatory base in Florida should enable GEMI to continue growing robustly. At current levels, I believe the stock offers attractive risk-reward with meaningful upside potential ahead.

data.title: Seeking Gold And Retirement Income? IGLD Is The Answer
data.description:
For high-yield investors, who are not only focused on yield, but also on the durability of it, the places to invest are inherently scarce. While there are a lot of instruments out there that have, say, 10%+ yields or that are underpinned by really solid fundamentals, when it comes to capturing both worlds in a single security, the opportunities are very limited. And it's not necessarily the "10%+" yield level that shrinks the investable universe of tangible and high-quality income plays. I would say that given the relatively recent base rate cuts (starting from mid-2024) and the direction of travel in future interest rates that has already been baked into the asset pricing models, finding quality investments from the 7% or 8% yield level has become extremely difficult.

So, investors, who still remain committed to maximizing yield while keeping the risk parameters acceptable (e.g., avoidance of excessive leverage, dividend cuts, NAV impairments), end up becoming concentrated within the following areas: MLPs (AMLP), real estate (VNQ), BDCs (BIZD), leveraged CEFs (PDO), covered calls and in some selected traditional common stock plays such as Pfizer Inc. (NYSE:PFE) and Altria (NYSE:MO).

Now, even though I am personally heavily invested in these areas, I have to say that the level of risk is not the lowest there. For example, MLPs are subject to increased geopolitical risks and declining commodity prices, BDCs are facing a tough battle with falling interest rates and spread compression, real estate is stagnating and exposed to risk of potentially increasing long-term yields, and covered calls are usually tied to the expensive S&P 500 (SPY) or Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) indices.

The dilemma that I and perhaps some other high-yield and quality focused income investors have is how to introduce extra protection and diversification without sacrificing the yield component.

Bonds (TLT) are really not an option because of relatively small yield and quite similar risk exposures to REITs (i.e., increasing long-term yields putting pressure on prices). Plus, the idea of locking in unattractive yield that has no inflation-protection element does not seem like a good one in the environment we are in now. Theoretically, a solution for this (sufficient income) could be the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (BATS:TLTW), but again, going long TLTW would just magnify the exposure to the unfavorable movements in the long-term segment of the bond market.

Gold (GLD) is, obviously, a great hedge against the main structural risks, but there is zero yield. However, here we have an interesting solution to consider - the FT Vest Gold Strategy Target Income ETF (BATS:IGLD). Let me explain.

Thesis

In a nutshell, IGLD's offering boils down to capturing both gold and income factors. I will start with the income side because that is more straightforward.

Currently, IGLD yields ~15.3%, which should definitely be attractive enough for most income investors to include the asset in their portfolios. The yield is even higher than for the typical (widely known) covered call ETFs such as the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI) and the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI) - yielding 8.4% and 13.5%, respectively. If we look at IGLD's historical average yield (excluding 2021, which is the inception year of ETF - it takes time for covered call ETFs to build up yield), then we still get an enticing level of ~9.3%.

I think that it would be fair to say that IGLD for sure qualifies for income-seeking investor portfolios.

Now, turning to the safety aspect. This is where we have to understand the underlying mechanics as well.

At its core, IGLD is a typical covered call ETF, where there is a specific asset class or index chosen as the underlying that is then made subject to a short call option program. The most common covered call ETFs are linked to QQQ, SPY or other similar (liquid) indices. Yet, in IGLD's case, GLD is established as the underlying factor against which covered call overlay strategy is introduced.

So, what does it mean?

First, the risk and return factors are quite standard (as for a covered call ETF). IGLD's upside potential is capped by the strike values associated with sold call options. The downside is almost fully left open with some minor protection stemming from the collection of premiums.

Second, and this is important, the total return figure is driven by GLD dynamics and its implied volatility. Namely, the more GLD rallies, the higher the returns coming from IGLD. If GLD falls, IGLD follows (to a slightly lesser extent).

The only scenario in which IGLD could outperform pure-play gold exposure is when the underlying index (i.e., GLD) trades sideways, declines in a gradual and linear fashion, or increases in a gradual and linear fashion. The chance of these kinds of pathways materializing in practice is quite limited. The reason is because the markets, and especially a volatile asset class as GLD, tend to evolve with significant volatility spikes (both to the downside and upside).

For example, in the chart below, we can see how IGLD has performed in a time period, when GLD was largely flat:

Even though in the 2021–2022 period GLD delivered just ~5.6% in returns (i.e., something we could label as flat performance), IGLD still underperformed because of the relatively elevated ups and downs.

Having said that, the way I see IGLD is not as an instrument to match or outperform standard gold exposure. Instead, I view it as a solution to diversify my income-oriented portfolio with a gold factor, while keeping the high-income criteria checked. To put it differently, I am completely fine with foregoing some percentage points in returns versus a pure-play investment in GLD as long as I can secure gold's value proposition in my portfolio in a fashion that is supportive for my overall strategy to build a passive income portfolio from which to fund my living expenses.

The bottom line

A large chunk of the asset universe is richly priced. Be it record-high QQQ, SPY, or historically tight credit spreads or even relatively depressed dividend yields for conventional income producing assets - almost everywhere we look, there are indeed some signs of aggressive pricing.

In my view, this kind of situation does not fit well in the prevailing economic environment, when we have a weakening labor market, an ongoing inflation battle, and huge risks from the geopolitical front etc.

On top of that, we have to appreciate the risk of fiscal deficits and the corresponding consequences on the dollar (which is already depressed) and long-term yield segment.

This is where gold comes into play very well - acting as a hedge to inflation and a potential meltdown in the dollar and/or high-risk asset markets.

Luckily, IGLD allows durable income-oriented investors to capture these benefits as well.

data.title: Fiduciary Management: A Few Holdings That We Find Compelling Over The Long Term
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Fiduciary Management Q3 2025 Investment Strategy Outlook

Years from now, there will be a more definitive view on 2025 relative to history. In the meantime, we stay the course and observe the following: economic growth is showing cracks, valuations are stretched, excess is abundant, the market is concentrated, AI exuberance is unrelenting, and investors are piling into equities at a record pace. Our portfolios look very different than our benchmarks, with a focus on idiosyncratic dislocations. With an eye toward business quality, balance sheet strength, and valuation, we will continue to proceed with caution. Downside protection has been a hallmark at FMI over our 45-year history. If volatility increases significantly and we find ourselves in a bear market, we will be poised to take advantage of more opportunistic valuations.

Listed below are a few portfolio holdings that we find compelling over the long term:

OneMain Holdings Inc. (OMF) – Small Cap/All Cap

OneMain is a scale leader in nonprime personal lending. Although they have a messy corporate history, our research suggests that in most key areas they are superior to their peers. We believe with a few years of clean results and an improved macro environment, this company could be reframed as a differentiated, high-quality financial. Given organic growth opportunities in personal lending, and newer areas such as credit card and direct auto, we believe they can grow organically in the mid-single digits with operating leverage, while at the same time returning significant capital through a large dividend and episodic buybacks. Although the current macro environment has stressed their core consumer and pressured net charge offs, we believe the income statement alone provides sufficient protection from an adverse outcome in nearly all reasonable scenarios, with further protection on the balance sheet in a tail risk scenario. The macro backdrop has given us the opportunity to own this industry leading business at an attractive valuation.

Becton, Dickinson & Co. (BDX) – Large Cap/All Cap/Global

Becton, Dickinson is a medical supply and device company, selling low-cost medical essentials. Products include vascular access devices, pre-fillable syringes, and catheters, to name a few. Becton is the leader in most of its product lines and often competes in oligopoly market structures. Their management team has been executing on a strategy to grow and simplify the business. This has been successful but is obscured by noise in the financials caused by the pandemic, portfolio actions, and other temporary factors, providing us with an opportunity to buy a quality business at an attractive price. We believe execution on the company’s growth strategy and pending business separation will unlock value, with management targeting over 5% annual organic revenue growth and continued margin expansion in the coming years. Manufacturing scale is a key competitive advantage, with the company manufacturing billions of devices annually. Driven by healthcare utilization, the business is durable. Post their separation of biosciences and diagnostics solutions business in 2026, ~90% of revenue is recurring in nature (consumables). The valuation is attractive given the company’s defensive attributes and growth potential.

Greggs PLC (OTCPK:GGGSF)[GRG LN] – International/Global

Greggs is a vertically integrated food-on-the-go operator with over 2,650 shops across the UK. Greggs sells products which are low ticket, consumed immediately, and often repurchased during the day. The company’s strong brand and differentiated business model have created loyal customers and long-term market share gains. Elevated cost inflation and growth investments have coincided with a demand slowdown, weighing on the near-term outlook and pressuring the stock. While sales growth and margins are likely to take a step back in the interim, given investments being made across the business (supply chain, innovation, employees, channels/ partnership), we think the company has a long runway for new store openings. The company is cresting the peak of their supply chain investment cycle this year, management remains disciplined, and they are in an excellent financial position with cash flow set to inflect thereafter. We view the valuation as too low relative to their long-term prospects.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cloudflare: Still No AI Boost Detected
data.description:
Cloudflare, Inc. (NYSE:NET) remains a perplexing AI cloud play with the business not showing the ability to boost growth during surging AI demand. The company has constantly maintained strong growth in the upper 20% range, but growth hasn't accelerated and doesn't appear headed in that direction. My investment thesis remains ultra Bearish on the stock as the ongoing rally prices in a lot of accelerating growth not in the cards.

Policing The Internet

The best way to describe Cloudflare is the "police of the internet" with the company providing a combination of network and security services for customers. The platform blocks billions of threats online and is trusted by millions of organizations.

While this all sounds promising from a business perspective, the stock has become detached from the reality of actual results. Cloudflare had slumped below $50 before the AI revolution boosted the stock to a very high elevation.

Since the ChatGPT launch about 3 years ago, Cloudflare has risen over 335% while revenue has only gained 87%. The stock has clearly lost touch with reality.

Cloudflare still grew at a very strong 28% clip in the last quarter with general annual growth forecast in the same range for the next few years. The key here is that the business was growing at a 42% clip back in Q4'22 and in those 3 years of the AI revolution sales growth has slipped to only 28% while actual big AI plays like Nvidia (NVDA) and other cloud providers have seen growth reach over 100% rates.

The AI demand situation is likely keeping sales growth closer to 30% rates when normal growth might have seen a deceleration to only 20%. Either way, the stock is priced for massive AI growth and it's just not occurring.

As the national business media starts debating on whether an AI bubble exists, an investor really needs to understand whether an AI related stock has truly rallied due to fundamentals and future growth or just hype. If not, one could end up like the crypto market where investors apparently made millions on risky alt coins using leverage and lost all of it with a simple dip when the stock market was only down 2% to 3%. The crypto market saw $19 billion in liquidations with some alt coins like DOGE down over 60% last Friday alone.

Cloudflare definitely has a viable connectivity cloud business, but the stock isn't based on fundamentals. The company regularly announces promising products like the recent Trusted Agent Protocol for leading payments companies to secure agentic commerce, but consensus estimates aren't forecasting any accelerated growth from these deals.

The company provides a lot of AI infrastructure making the business valuable to the payment processors like Visa (V) and MasterCard (MA), but it doesn't always mean that Cloudflare gets game changing growth. In fact, consensus analyst estimates have revenues actually still below the original estimates back in mid-2023.

Priced Beyond Perfection

Cloudflare was forecast to report 2027 revenues of $3.8 billion back in August 2023 and the consensus estimate now is only $3.4 billion. Over the last 2 years, analysts have cut revenue estimates by $0.4 billion.

The market clearly is ignoring this perspective. Cloudflare is an important network and security tool for AI, but customers aren't paying aggressively to expand services.

Cloudflare has seen the market valuation soar to $82.5 billion with 370 million diluted shares outstanding. The stock now trades at an eye watering 31x 2026 sales targets of $2.7 billion.

The company even promotes the strong balance sheet with a cash balance of nearly $4 billion now, but the amount is so small relative to the fully diluted market cap. Even more important, Cloudflare only has a net cash balance below $1 billion due to $3.3 billion in convertible debt.

The stock trades at 195x 2026 EPS targets of only $1.12. Cloudflare even has a targeted 13% to 14% operating margin with a long-term model of 20%+. The company isn't forecasting any major leverage boost during future strong growth, such as the stock already trading at ~132x normalized operating margins, leading to about 50% EPS growth.

Cloudflare is a great company with a bright future due to AI platform demand, but the business just isn't built to see accelerating growth. The stock is already priced for perfection and won't trade down to 10x sales until 2031 and only trades at 90x 2029 EPS targets of $2.47, with no analysts even providing forecasted EPS targets out far enough to where the stock becomes a solid value.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Cloudflare isn't likely facing a full crypto scenario, but investors need to understand the stock is priced for a major reset similar to what happened to crypto investors that didn't take any unjustified profits. Investors should use the ongoing rise in stock price to lock in profits on Cloudflare.
